nativeRange.habitat: Highland streams, hill streams, and slow-moving lowland waters of Java and Sumatra; typically clear, shaded streams with leaf litter, submerged roots, and dense riparian vegetation; rice paddies also reported

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java fern, Microsorum, Taxiphyllum, Cryptocoryne, Anubias)
clay pots or PVC piping as cave-style shelters
floating plants (Pistia, Salvinia) to diffuse light

diet.notes: Naturally feeds on aquatic and terrestrial invertebrates. Captive specimens should receive small live or frozen foods regularly — Daphnia, Artemia, chironomid larvae (bloodworm), and micro-worms are all accepted. Dried micro-pellets and flake are taken once recognised as food but should not form the sole diet. Prone to obesity; avoid overfeeding. Feed small amounts 1–2 times daily.

goodWith: small peaceful cyprinids from similar habitats (e.g., Rasbora, Boraras)
small non-fin-nipping tetras in appropriately soft water
other small peaceful wild bettas from compatible water requirements
avoidWith: larger or aggressive fish that will intimidate or harass
boisterous or fast-moving tankmates that stress cryptic species
other male Betta picta without sufficient space and sight-breaks

summary: Betta picta is the type species of the picta group and the smallest mouthbrooding Betta, reaching ~5 cm. Native to Java and Sumatra, it is the hardiest wild Betta — tolerating broader water parameters than most congeners — and a paternal mouthbrooder that spawns readily in captivity.
careNotes:
Betta picta is the type species of the Betta picta complex, a group of small paternal mouthbrooding wild bettas native to Java and Sumatra, Indonesia. At around 5 cm it is among the smallest members of the genus, and is regarded as the hardiest wild Betta — tolerating a wider temperature and pH band than most congeners. It is IUCN Near Threatened in the wild due to habitat loss.

A 60 × 30 cm tank (approx. 54 L) is adequate for a pair or small species-only group. The species is cryptic and shy; a well-planted setup with driftwood, leaf litter, clay-pot caves, and floating plants is essential to see natural behaviour. Keep lighting subdued. Use a gentle sponge filter — the species comes from sluggish waters and strong flow will stress it. A tight-fitting lid is mandatory; like all bettas it is an excellent jumper and also requires access to humid air above the water surface for its labyrinth organ.

Soft, slightly acidic water is preferred but not as strictly required as for many wild bettas — pH 5.5–7.5 and gH 1–12 are workable, making it more forgiving in tap-water areas. Temperature can range 22–28°C, with the lower-mid end (22–25°C) reflecting its highland stream origins. The species is facultatively air-breathing.

Betta picta is classified intermediate primarily because of its water-quality sensitivity, cryptic feeding behaviour, and the specialised setup (species-only or carefully chosen companions, soft water, dense decor, tight lid) it requires — rather than being inherently difficult. It is not suitable for a typical community tank. Male conspecific aggression is moderate; keep pairs or small groups with ample sight-breaks.
breedingNotes: Betta picta is a paternal mouthbrooder and one of the smallest mouthbrooding bettas. Spawning is readily triggered in captivity, often by a water change. After an embracing spawn, the male collects and incubates the fertilised eggs orally in his buccal cavity for approximately 9–12 days until the fry are free-swimming and fully formed. During brooding the male stops feeding; do not disturb him. The female should be separated or the tank provided with adequate hiding so she is not harassed post-spawn. A brood typically numbers 10–40 fry. Released fry are large enough to accept Artemia nauplii and micro-worm immediately. The species spawns en masse (multiple pairs releasing simultaneously in the wild), an adaptation thought to reduce individual predation risk; in captivity single-pair spawns are standard. A tight lid and surface access to humid air is critical for fry developing their labyrinth organs.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Betta picta
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided tank dimensions (60×30 cm), water parameters (22–28°C, pH 5.5–7.5, hardness 18–90 ppm), adult size (40–50 mm), diet (invertebrates; live/frozen preferred), breeding (paternal mouthbrooder, 9–12 day incubation), decor preferences, native range (western/central Java), and compatibility guidance (species-only or very peaceful companions).

additional.name: FishBase — Betta picta
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 7.2 cm SL, used 5.5 cm as practical aquarium adult size), temperature 22–24°C, pH 6.5–7.5, hardness upper limit ~22 dH, family Osphronemidae, order Anabantiformes, native to Indonesia. Notes carnivore trophic level 3.3, facultative air-breathing, IUCN Near Threatened.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Spotted betta
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Valenciennes, 1846), native range (highland streams of Java, Sumatra), adult size ~6 cm, paternal mouthbrooding behaviour, mass-spawning adaptation, IUCN Near Threatened status, common name "Java fighting fish".

additional.name: AKA Wild Betta Blog — Betta picta: The Forgotten Fighter of Java’s Highlands
additional.notes: Supplementary hobbyist source. Confirmed temperature 23–26°C, pH 6.0–6.5, GH ~10°, adult size up to 6.5 cm TL, paternal mouthbrooding, specific Java localities (Mojokerto, Madura, Candi, Madiun, Ambarawa) and western Sumatra (Lake Singkarak area). Notes dense vegetation and submerged roots as preferred decor.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Species groups within the genus Betta
additional.notes: Confirmed Betta picta as the type/name-giving species of the picta group (small paternal mouthbrooding wild bettas); group distinguished by rounded unpaired fins, dark distal fin margins, pre- and post-orbital stripes, and iridescent opercle scales.

fish.breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder; spawning readily triggered by water changes. Male incubates eggs orally ~9–12 days; brood 10–40 fry. Released fry take Artemia nauplii immediately. Separate female post-spawn to avoid harassment. Stop feeding male during brooding.
